serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Crispy Shredded Shiitake is approximately 50 g, equivalent to a small snack bowl or a side portion as a cold dish appetizer.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, shiitake, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168436); supplemented with estimated frying-oil absorption and seasoning additions based on USDA FoodData Central reference data for deep-fried mushroom preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for deep-fried shredded shiitake snack. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried shiitake mushroom data (FDC ID 168436) with estimated adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption (approximately 20–25 g fat per 100 g final product), cornstarch coating contribution to carbohydrates, and seasoning sodium contribution. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 30% of total fat based on typical vegetable frying oil profile. All values are estimates and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed mushroom and starch-coated fried snack — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy deep-fried shredded shiitake. GI of 45 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high dietary fibre content of shiitake mushrooms (8 g/100 g) which attenuates glycaemic response; (2) fat content from deep-frying which slows gastric emptying; (3) cornstarch coating contributing moderate starch load. Reference: Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) for fried starchy snacks with high fibre. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (45 × 12.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (580 mg/100 g; 290 mg per 50 g serving) from seasoning blend
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this snack due to its high sodium content; a single 50 g serving provides approximately 12% of the 2,400 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (estimated 45) partially offset by high dietary fibre (8 g/100 g) and fat-slowed absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume small portions (≤50 g) occasionally, as the low estimated GI and high fibre content moderate the glycaemic response, but the high fat content and added sugar in the seasoning require portion control and monitoring of total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (28.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium (580 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried snack due to its high total fat and sodium content, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; air-fried or oven-baked preparation methods would substantially reduce fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying with vegetable oils high in omega-6 fatty acids may contribute to pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit deep-fried foods including this snack, as high omega-6 vegetable oil content may exacerbate systemic inflammation; occasional small portions are unlikely to cause significant harm.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Shredded Shiitake (香酥香菇丝, Xiāngsū xiānggūsī) is a popular Chinese vegetarian snack and cold dish appetizer made by thinly shredding fresh or rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, lightly coating them in cornstarch or flour, and deep-frying them until golden and crunchy. While still hot, the strips are tossed in a savory-sweet seasoning blend of five-spice powder, salt, sugar, and optional chili flakes. The deep-frying process removes most of the moisture, concentrating the umami flavor of the shiitake and producing a satisfyingly crunchy yet slightly chewy texture that closely resembles beef jerky, making it a popular plant-based alternative. It is commonly served as a liang-cai (cold dish) in Chinese restaurants and is widely prepared as a home snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 380 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 28.5 g fat, 24.5 g carbohydrates, and 8 g dietary fibre. The high fibre content from shiitake mushrooms and the fat from frying result in a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45. However, the sodium content of 580 mg/100 g is clinically significant, making this snack unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease in large quantities. The high total fat content also warrants caution for those managing cardiovascular risk or caloric intake. Shiitake mushrooms are a natural source of B vitamins including riboflavin, niacin, and B6, as well as vitamin D, selenium, and potassium.
common_user_queries: Is crispy shiitake mushroom snack healthy?
How many calories in crispy shiitake strips?
Is mushroom jerky good for diabetics?
Can I eat crispy shiitake if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of fried mushroom snacks?
How is crispy shredded shiitake made?
meal_context: vegetarian topping for rice or congee

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried shredded shiitake snack exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried shiitake mushroom data with estimated adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, cornstarch coating, and seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried shiitake mushroom (FDC ID 168436) with estimated adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and seasoning; no exact approved-source entry exists for this preparation. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 3.5 g/100 g based on approximately 30% saturated fat fraction of typical vegetable frying oil (e.g., soybean or palm oil blend); this is an estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this deep-fried preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-17. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — shiitake mushrooms are a plant food containing no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for shiitake mushrooms). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — deep-frying destroys residual vitamin C). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-17. 6. This is a Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null. 7. The input source 'Journal of Food Processing and Preservation' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; all nutrient values are grounded in USDA FoodData Central. 8. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 50 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 50 g default serving.
